brief_description: Poland's former royal capital and one of Europe's best-preserved medieval cities, Kraków escaped the destruction of World War II with its old town largely intact. The UNESCO-listed Stare Miasto unfolds around Rynek Główny, the largest medieval market square in Europe, while Wawel Hill crowns the city with the royal castle and cathedral of Polish kings. Beyond the Gothic spires lies Kazimierz, the soulful former Jewish quarter, and a living, affordable city of milk bars, craft beer, and obwarzanek pretzel-rings sold from street carts.
subtitle: A Thousand Years of Polish History

sections.title: Why Kraków
sections.content: Kraków is Poland's cultural and historic heart—the seat of kings for over five centuries and a city that survived World War II with its medieval core remarkably intact. The UNESCO-listed Old Town offers a continuous sweep of Gothic, Renaissance, and Baroque architecture, anchored by Rynek Główny, the largest medieval market square in Europe. This is the real thing, not a reconstruction: the Cloth Hall has traded since the 14th century, the hejnał trumpet call still sounds hourly from St. Mary's Basilica, and Jagiellonian University has taught since 1364. Add a soulful Jewish quarter, sobering WWII memory sites, and genuine value for money, and Kraków rewards far more than a weekend.

sections.title: The Neighbourhoods
sections.content: Stare Miasto (the Old Town) is the walkable medieval core, ringed by the green Planty park where the city walls once stood and entered through the surviving St. Florian's Gate and Barbican. Wawel Hill rises at its southern edge above the Vistula, home to the royal castle and cathedral. South of the centre, Kazimierz was for centuries the Jewish quarter—its Old and Remuh synagogues, Plac Nowy zapiekanka stalls, and atmospheric bars and cafés make it the city's most characterful district. Across the river, Podgórze holds the WWII ghetto sites, Ghetto Heroes' Square, and Oskar Schindler's Enamel Factory. Further out, Nowa Huta is a planned socialist-realist district worth a tram ride for its stark contrast.

sections.title: Getting Around
sections.content: Kraków's historic core is compact and best explored on foot—cars are largely kept out of the Old Town, and the Planty ring loops the centre in a pleasant walk. An efficient network of trams and buses, run by MPK, covers everything beyond, including Podgórze, Kazimierz, and Nowa Huta; buy tickets from machines, drivers, or apps like Jakdojade and validate on board. From John Paul II Kraków–Balice Airport (KRK), about 11 km west, a train reaches Kraków Główny station in roughly 21 minutes. Taxis and ride-hailing apps such as Bolt and Uber are inexpensive for late-night trips.

why_visit.intro: Kraków packs a millennium of Polish history into one of Europe's most walkable and affordable capitals. The royal castle on Wawel Hill, the vast medieval Market Square, and the bohemian former Jewish quarter of Kazimierz all sit within an easy stroll of one another. The sobering memory of the 20th century—Schindler's Factory, the wartime ghetto, and Auschwitz beyond—gives the city extraordinary depth, while some of the best food and liveliest bars in Central Europe reward a long, unhurried visit.

highlights.title: Wawel & the Royal Hill
highlights.content: Wawel Royal Castle and its cathedral crown the city above the Vistula, the coronation and burial place of Polish monarchs. Wander the Renaissance arcaded courtyard, climb Sigismund's Tower to its great bell, descend to the royal crypts, and take in the legend of the Wawel Dragon below the hill.

highlights.title: Rynek Główny & St. Mary's Basilica
highlights.content: Rynek Główny is the largest medieval market square in Europe, anchored by the 14th-century Cloth Hall (Sukiennice) and the Gothic St. Mary's Basilica, home to Veit Stoss's towering carved altarpiece and the hourly hejnał trumpet call. Below the square, the Rynek Underground Museum reveals the medieval town in situ.

highlights.title: Kazimierz & Jewish Heritage
highlights.content: The former Jewish quarter of Kazimierz preserves centuries of heritage in its Old and Remuh synagogues and the Galicia Jewish Museum, while Plac Nowy buzzes with zapiekanka stalls and lively bars like Alchemia. The district hosts the renowned Jewish Culture Festival each summer.

highlights.title: Schindler's Factory & WWII History
highlights.content: Across the river in Podgórze, Oskar Schindler's Enamel Factory is now a powerful museum of Kraków under Nazi occupation. Nearby, Ghetto Heroes' Square (Plac Bohaterów Getta), with its memorial of empty chairs, marks the site of the wartime ghetto.

highlights.title: Day Trips: Wieliczka, Auschwitz & Zakopane
highlights.content: Kraków is the gateway to extraordinary excursions: the UNESCO-listed Wieliczka Salt Mine with its subterranean chapels just 14 km away; the Auschwitz-Birkenau Memorial at Oświęcim, around 66 km west; and the Tatra Mountains and resort town of Zakopane, about 100 km south for hiking and highland culture.

highlights.title: Food & Milk-Bar Culture
highlights.content: Eat your way through obwarzanek krakowski pretzel-rings from street carts, pierogi, żurek, and bigos, with smoky oscypek cheese and Plac Nowy zapiekanki. Budget bar mleczny (milk bars) serve home-style Polish food, while restaurants from Pod Aniołami to Michelin-starred Bottiglieria 1881 showcase the city's range, alongside a thriving vodka and craft-beer scene.

practical_info.language: Polish (English widely spoken in tourist areas)
practical_info.tipping: 10% at restaurants; round up at bars and for taxis
practical_info.tap_water: Safe to drink—Kraków's tap water meets EU standards

airport_options: John Paul II Kraków–Balice (KRK) — ~11 km west; train to Kraków Główny in ~21 min

seasonal_notes.spring: Blooming Planty park, Easter traditions like Rękawka, pleasant 9-20°C temperatures, manageable crowds
seasonal_notes.summer: Long days, Vistula boulevards, festivals like the Jewish Culture Festival, but peak tourist season
seasonal_notes.fall: Golden parks, Unsound and Conrad festivals, fewer crowds, crisp air—a fine time to visit
seasonal_notes.winter: The Christmas Market on Rynek Główny, the UNESCO szopki nativity-crib contest, cold but atmospheric

best_time_to_visit.overall: May-June and September for best weather and manageable crowds
best_time_to_visit.budget: November-March (excluding Christmas/New Year) for lowest prices
best_time_to_visit.weather: June-August for warmest weather and longest days (~16.5h of daylight in June)
best_time_to_visit.events: Jewish Culture Festival (late June-early July), Unsound (October), Christmas Market (December)

Must Do.cost: Free grounds; 35-50 PLN for exhibition tickets

Must Do.optimal_time: Early morning or evening when lit
On the hour to hear the trumpet call

Best in Winter.activity: Café Camelot for coffee and cake

Local Experiences.activity: Craft beer or vodka at Starka in Kazimierz
